name: Venezuela
tagline: From the indigenous peoples of the Orinoco and the coast Columbus reached in 1498, through the Spanish 'little Venice' and the Captaincy General of Caracas, to the cradle of South American independence, the petro-state built on Lake Maracaibo oil, and the Bolivarian Revolution and crisis of the present.
summary: Venezuela's history begins with the indigenous Carib, Arawak and Timoto-Cuica peoples and with Christopher Columbus's third voyage of 1498, when he reached the Gulf of Paria and the Orinoco delta, the only South American mainland he ever touched. The following year navigators of the Ojeda expedition, struck by stilt dwellings on Lake Maracaibo, named the land 'Venezuela', or 'little Venice'; Caracas was founded in 1567 and the Captaincy General of Venezuela created in 1777. Venezuela became the cradle of Spanish American independence: Francisco de Miranda's movement led to the first declaration of independence in the region on 5 July 1811, and Simon Bolivar's campaigns, sealed at the Battle of Carabobo in 1821, freed the country, which formed part of Gran Colombia until it separated in 1830 under Jose Antonio Paez. A century of caudillos and the Federal War gave way to transformation by oil from the 1920s, a democratic era after 1958, and then the Bolivarian Revolution of Hugo Chavez from 1999 and a deep economic and humanitarian crisis under Nicolas Maduro that, by United Nations estimates, has driven millions abroad.

founding.label: Declaration of independence from Spain
founding.detail: On 5 July 1811 a congress at Caracas declared Venezuela independent of Spain, the first formal declaration of independence in Spanish America, establishing the short-lived First Republic. The break followed the deposition of the Spanish captain general in April 1810 after Napoleon's occupation of Spain shattered royal authority, and the return of the revolutionary Francisco de Miranda, who took command of the new state. The First Republic soon collapsed, and independence had to be won over a decade of war; it was finally secured by Simon Bolivar's decisive victory at the Battle of Carabobo on 24 June 1821.

quickFacts.label: Founding peoples
quickFacts.value: Carib, Arawak and Timoto-Cuica peoples of the coast, plains and Andes

quickFacts.label: Reached by Columbus
quickFacts.value: 1498, on his third voyage, at the Gulf of Paria and the Orinoco

quickFacts.label: Caracas founded
quickFacts.value: 1567, by Diego de Losada, as Santiago de Leon de Caracas

quickFacts.label: Independence declared
quickFacts.value: 5 July 1811, the first in Spanish America; secured at Carabobo in 1821

quickFacts.label: Transformed by oil
quickFacts.value: From the 1920s, on the fields of Lake Maracaibo

eras.title: Indigenous Peoples and the Encounter of 1498
eras.standfirst: For thousands of years the land was home to Carib, Arawak and Timoto-Cuica peoples, until Christopher Columbus reached the Gulf of Paria in 1498, the only South American mainland he set foot on, and Spanish navigators named the coast 'little Venice'.
body: Long before any European sail appeared, the territory of present-day Venezuela was inhabited by a diversity of indigenous peoples. Along the Caribbean coast and the Orinoco lived Carib and Arawak (Arawak-speaking) peoples, fishers, farmers and traders, while in the Andean highlands to the west the Timoto-Cuica built settled communities that cultivated terraced fields and irrigated crops. The territory lay outside the orbit of the great Andean and Mesoamerican empires, and for that reason the Spanish would later regard it as a marginal corner of their American dominions.

European contact came on Columbus's third voyage. In early August 1498 his ships entered the Gulf of Paria, near the mouth of the Orinoco, and Columbus, astonished by the great flow of fresh water pouring into the sea, became the first European to set foot on the South American mainland, a coast he believed bordered an earthly paradise. The encounter opened the region to Spanish exploration and, soon after, to conquest, displacement and disease that would devastate the indigenous population over the following century.

The land took its name from these first years of contact. In 1499 an expedition under Alonso de Ojeda, on which the Florentine navigator Amerigo Vespucci sailed, explored the western coast and Lake Maracaibo; the indigenous houses built on piles over the water are said to have reminded the voyagers of Venice, and they called the place Venezuela, 'little Venice'. The first lasting Spanish town on the South American mainland followed in 1527 with the founding of Coro, whose early earthen-built quarter survives as a World Heritage site.

events.title: Indigenous peoples of coast, plains and Andes
events.summary: Carib and Arawak peoples settle the Caribbean coast and the Orinoco basin, while the Timoto-Cuica farm the western Andes, long before European contact.

events.title: Columbus reaches the Gulf of Paria
events.summary: On his third voyage Christopher Columbus enters the Gulf of Paria near the Orinoco, the only South American mainland he ever set foot on.

events.title: The naming of 'little Venice'
events.summary: Navigators of the Ojeda expedition, on which Amerigo Vespucci sailed, name the coast around Lake Maracaibo 'Venezuela', or 'little Venice', after its stilt dwellings.

events.title: Founding of Coro
events.summary: Coro is founded as one of the first lasting Spanish towns on the mainland; its earthen colonial quarter is later inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage site.

pullquote.text: It was the first locale in which Christopher Columbus set foot on the mainland of the New World.
pullquote.attribution: Library of Congress, Venezuela: A Country Study, on the encounter of 1498

eras.title: Colonial Venezuela and the Captaincy General
eras.standfirst: For nearly three centuries Venezuela was a peripheral province of Spanish America; Caracas was founded in 1567, a cacao-and-slave economy took root along the coast, and in 1777 the scattered provinces were united into the Captaincy General of Venezuela.
body: Spanish settlement spread slowly westward and into the interior through the sixteenth century. In 1567 Diego de Losada founded Santiago de Leon de Caracas in a fertile coastal valley, and the town gradually became the centre of colonial life. Venezuela had little of the silver that made Mexico and Peru rich, and so remained a backwater of the empire, governed for much of the period from the distant audiencia of Santo Domingo and then Santa Fe de Bogota, its provinces only loosely tied together.

The colony's wealth came instead from agriculture, above all cacao, grown on coastal plantations worked increasingly by enslaved Africans, whose forced migration added a third great strand to the indigenous and Spanish populations and shaped the society of the coast. A creole planter elite, the so-called mantuanos, grew prosperous and proud on the cacao trade, while resentment of Spanish commercial monopoly, taxation and the privileges of peninsular-born officials slowly mounted among them.

In 1777 the Spanish crown drew the separate provinces together into a single administrative unit, the Captaincy General of Venezuela, with Caracas as its capital, a step that for the first time gave the territory the rough shape of the future nation. The reforms of the late eighteenth century, the example of the American and French revolutions, and the ferment of Enlightenment ideas among the creole elite all prepared the ground for revolt. When Napoleon deposed the Spanish king in 1808, the crisis of royal legitimacy reached the New World, and Venezuela would be among the first to act.

events.title: Founding of Caracas
events.summary: Diego de Losada founds Santiago de Leon de Caracas, which grows into the colony's principal city and the future national capital.

events.title: A cacao-and-slave economy
events.summary: Coastal plantations growing cacao, worked by enslaved Africans, enrich a creole planter elite, the mantuanos, and shape colonial society.

events.title: Captaincy General of Venezuela created
events.summary: A royal decree unites the scattered provinces into the Captaincy General of Venezuela, with Caracas as capital, giving the territory the outline of the future nation.

pullquote.text: Venezuela was of only marginal consequence within the Spanish American empire during most of the next three centuries.
pullquote.attribution: Library of Congress, Venezuela: A Country Study

eras.title: The Cradle of Independence and Simon Bolivar
eras.standfirst: Venezuela led Spanish America into independence: Francisco de Miranda's movement produced the first declaration of independence in the region in 1811, and Simon Bolivar's long campaigns, sealed at Carabobo in 1821, liberated the country within the union of Gran Colombia, from which it separated in 1830.
body: On 19 April 1810 the cabildo of Caracas deposed the Spanish captain general and set up a governing junta, and the revolutionary Francisco de Miranda, the 'Precursor' who had spent decades seeking foreign aid for the cause, returned home. A congress declared independence on 5 July 1811, the first formal declaration in Spanish America, and a constitution founded the First Republic. But the new state was fragile; a devastating earthquake in 1812 and royalist resistance brought its collapse, Miranda was handed to the Spanish and died in a Cadiz prison in 1816, and independence had to be fought for through years of brutal war.

Leadership passed to Simon Bolivar, the Caracas-born aristocrat who became the foremost soldier and statesman of South American liberation. Driven into exile and back, he waged campaigns of extraordinary daring, declaring a war 'to the death' against Spanish rule, crossing the Andes to liberate New Granada at Boyaca in 1819, and proclaiming the union of Gran Colombia. The decisive blow in Venezuela fell on 24 June 1821 at the Battle of Carabobo, west of Caracas, where Bolivar, with the llanero cavalry of Jose Antonio Paez and British and Irish volunteers, routed the Spanish army and effectively freed the country.

For most of the 1820s Venezuela formed part of Bolivar's Gran Colombia, the great union of Venezuela, New Granada and Quito (modern Colombia, Ecuador and Panama). The union proved unworkable across such distances and rivalries, and as Bolivar's authority waned, the Venezuelan general Jose Antonio Paez led a separatist movement. In 1830, the year Bolivar died disillusioned near Santa Marta, a congress at Valencia declared Venezuela a separate, independent republic, with Paez as its first president and dominant figure.

events.title: Junta deposes the captain general
events.summary: The cabildo of Caracas removes the Spanish captain general and forms a governing junta, the opening act of the independence movement.

events.title: First declaration of independence in Spanish America
events.summary: A congress at Caracas declares Venezuela independent of Spain and founds the First Republic, the first such declaration in the region.

events.title: Death of Francisco de Miranda
events.summary: The 'Precursor' of independence, handed to the Spanish after the fall of the First Republic, dies a prisoner in a fortress at Cadiz.

events.title: Battle of Carabobo
events.summary: Bolivar's victory over the Spanish army at Carabobo, won with Paez's llaneros and foreign volunteers, virtually secures Venezuelan independence.

events.title: Separation from Gran Colombia
events.summary: As Gran Colombia dissolves and Bolivar dies, Jose Antonio Paez leads Venezuela to declare itself a separate republic and becomes its first president.

figures.name: Francisco de Miranda
figures.blurb: Caracas-born revolutionary known as El Precursor, who spent decades seeking foreign support for Spanish American independence, led the First Republic in 1811-1812, and died a Spanish prisoner in Cadiz.

figures.name: Simon Bolivar
figures.blurb: The towering figure of South American independence, born to a Caracas aristocratic family, who liberated Venezuela, New Granada, Quito, Peru and Bolivia and founded the union of Gran Colombia before dying disillusioned in 1830.

figures.name: Jose Antonio Paez
figures.blurb: Commander of the llanero plainsmen whose cavalry helped win Carabobo, he led Venezuela's separation from Gran Colombia in 1830 and became its first president and a dominant caudillo of the early republic.

pullquote.text: Venezuela became the first Spanish American colony to declare its independence, on 5 July 1811.

eras.title: Caudillos, the Federal War and the Rise of Oil
eras.standfirst: The nineteenth century was an age of caudillos and civil strife, above all the Federal War of 1859 to 1863; near its close the discovery of oil on Lake Maracaibo, exploited under the long dictatorship of Juan Vicente Gomez, began to remake Venezuela into a petro-state.
body: The republic founded in 1830 was dominated for decades by regional strongmen, the caudillos, who rose through personal armies and loyalties rather than stable institutions. Jose Antonio Paez led its first years, but the century brought recurring conflict between Conservative centralists, who favoured a strong unitary state, and Liberal federalists, who demanded provincial autonomy and social reform. The deepest of these struggles was the Federal War of 1859 to 1863, the bloodiest civil conflict since independence, which devastated the countryside and ended with the Treaty of Coche and a nominally federal constitution.

Out of the war's aftermath rose the Liberal caudillo Antonio Guzman Blanco, who governed for much of the 1870s and 1880s, modernising Caracas, building railways and roads, secularising the state and centralising power even under federal forms. Yet Venezuela remained a poor, largely rural country exporting coffee and cacao, its politics still turning on the rivalries of regional strongmen and its government chronically short of money, until a new source of wealth was found beneath its soil and lakes.

From 1908 the country fell under the iron rule of Juan Vicente Gomez, who held power until his death in 1935. During his dictatorship the discovery of oil transformed the nation: a great gusher at La Rosa on Lake Maracaibo in 1922 announced enormous reserves, and Gomez opened the fields to foreign companies. Production soared through the 1920s, and by the end of the decade Venezuela had become one of the world's leading oil exporters, its public finances and its future suddenly bound to a single commodity, with all the wealth and distortion that would follow.

events.title: The Federal War
events.summary: A devastating civil war between Liberal federalists and Conservative centralists ravages the country and ends with the Treaty of Coche and a federal constitution.

events.title: The era of Guzman Blanco
events.summary: The Liberal caudillo Antonio Guzman Blanco dominates Venezuela, modernising Caracas and centralising power while ruling through personal authority.

events.title: The dictatorship of Juan Vicente Gomez
events.summary: Gomez seizes power in 1908 and rules until 1935, presiding over the rise of the oil industry while suppressing opposition.

events.title: Oil gusher at Lake Maracaibo
events.summary: A massive blowout at La Rosa on Lake Maracaibo reveals vast reserves; foreign companies pour in and by 1929 Venezuela is a leading oil exporter.

figures.name: Juan Vicente Gomez
figures.blurb: Ruler of Venezuela from 1908 until his death in 1935, whose harsh dictatorship coincided with the rise of the oil industry that he opened to foreign companies, transforming the nation's economy.

pullquote.text: The discovery of oil at Lake Maracaibo turned a poor agricultural republic into one of the world's great petro-states.
pullquote.attribution: On the oil boom of the 1920s

eras.title: The Petro-State and the Democratic Era
eras.standfirst: Oil wealth and dictatorship gave way after 1958 to a durable democracy founded on the Punto Fijo power-sharing pact; for a generation Venezuela was a prosperous, stable exception in the region, until the collapse of oil prices and a debt crisis discredited its political order.
body: After Gomez died in 1935 Venezuela moved haltingly toward more open politics, with a brief democratic opening in the 1940s cut short by a coup. From 1948 the country was again ruled by the military, and from 1952 by the dictatorship of Marcos Perez Jimenez, who spent oil revenues on grand public works while repressing dissent. On 23 January 1958 a civil-military movement drove him from power, opening the longest period of democratic government in the nation's history.

To safeguard the new democracy, the leaders of the main parties signed the Punto Fijo pact in October 1958, agreeing to respect election results and share power among themselves. Romulo Betancourt, often called the father of Venezuelan democracy, won the ensuing election, and for four decades the two dominant parties, Accion Democratica and COPEI, alternated in office. Buoyed by the oil boom and the nationalisation of the industry in 1976, which created the state company Petroleos de Venezuela, the country enjoyed relative wealth and stability that made it a regional showcase for democracy.

The prosperity rested on a single, volatile commodity. When world oil prices collapsed in the 1980s, Venezuela was plunged into debt, inflation and falling living standards, and the gulf between the oil-rich elite and the urban poor widened. Austerity measures in 1989 set off the Caracazo, days of rioting in Caracas violently suppressed with heavy loss of life. Two attempted military coups in 1992, one led by a young army officer named Hugo Chavez, expressed the depth of popular anger, and by the late 1990s the discredited two-party order was collapsing.

events.title: Fall of Perez Jimenez and the Punto Fijo pact
events.summary: A civil-military uprising ousts the dictator Marcos Perez Jimenez; party leaders sign the Punto Fijo pact, founding four decades of power-sharing democracy.

events.title: Nationalisation of the oil industry
events.summary: Venezuela nationalises its oil industry and creates the state company Petroleos de Venezuela (PDVSA), the financial engine of the petro-state.

events.title: The Caracazo
events.summary: Austerity measures amid economic crisis trigger days of rioting in Caracas that are violently suppressed, with heavy loss of life, shaking the political order.

events.title: Chavez's failed coup
events.summary: Lieutenant Colonel Hugo Chavez leads a failed military coup against the government; jailed and later pardoned, he becomes a national political figure.

figures.name: Romulo Betancourt
figures.blurb: Founder of Accion Democratica and central architect of the post-1958 democratic order, elected president in the wake of the Punto Fijo pact and remembered as the father of Venezuelan democracy.

pullquote.text: For a generation after 1958, oil wealth made Venezuela a showcase of stable democracy in a region of coups.

eras.title: The Bolivarian Revolution and the Crisis
eras.standfirst: Hugo Chavez's election in 1998 launched the Bolivarian Revolution, a socialist project financed by oil; after his death in 2013 his successor Nicolas Maduro presided over an economic collapse and, by United Nations estimates, the largest exodus of people in the recent history of the Americas.
body: Hugo Chavez, the officer who had led the 1992 coup, was elected president in December 1998 on a promise to sweep away the old order. In 1999 a new constitution renamed the country the Bolivarian Republic of Venezuela and inaugurated what Chavez called the Bolivarian Revolution, a programme of nationalism, an enlarged state role in the economy, and ambitious social missions in health, education and housing funded by high oil prices. Chavez was a polarising figure: his supporters credited him with reducing poverty and empowering the poor, while critics charged him with concentrating power, weakening institutions and undermining the press and the courts. He survived a brief coup against him in 2002 and won repeated elections until his death from cancer in March 2013.

Power passed to his chosen successor, Nicolas Maduro, narrowly elected in 2013. Under Maduro the economy, already dependent on oil for the overwhelming share of its export earnings, entered a catastrophic decline as crude prices fell, output collapsed and the state printed money to cover its deficits. Venezuela suffered one of the worst peacetime economic contractions on record and a prolonged bout of hyperinflation, with severe shortages of food, medicine and basic goods. The political conflict deepened: in 2019 opposition leader Juan Guaido, as head of the National Assembly, declared himself interim president and was recognised by dozens of governments, while Maduro, backed by the military and by Russia, China and others, retained control of the state.

The human consequences have been measured chiefly by the United Nations and its refugee agency. According to UNHCR and the inter-agency R4V platform, by late 2024 more than 7.7 million Venezuelans had left the country, the great majority resettling elsewhere in Latin America and the Caribbean, in one of the largest displacement crises in the world. Maduro was declared the winner of the disputed July 2024 election by the national electoral authority, a result rejected by the opposition, which said its tally sheets showed its candidate Edmundo Gonzalez Urrutia had won, and questioned by the United States and many Latin American governments. As of 2026 Venezuela remains in a protracted political, economic and humanitarian crisis whose contested claims this account reports without endorsing any side.

events.title: Chavez elected president
events.summary: Hugo Chavez wins the presidency promising to sweep aside the old two-party order, launching the Bolivarian project.

events.title: The Bolivarian Constitution
events.summary: A new constitution, approved by referendum, renames the country the Bolivarian Republic of Venezuela and enlarges presidential and state power.

events.title: Death of Chavez; Maduro elected
events.summary: Hugo Chavez dies of cancer in March 2013, and his chosen successor Nicolas Maduro narrowly wins the ensuing election.

events.title: Economic collapse and exodus
events.summary: A deep contraction, hyperinflation and shortages drive a mass emigration that, by UN estimates, exceeds 7.7 million people by late 2024.

events.title: Disputed interim presidency
events.summary: Opposition leader Juan Guaido declares himself interim president and is recognised by dozens of states, while Maduro retains control of the government.

events.title: Disputed presidential election
events.summary: The electoral authority declares Maduro re-elected; the opposition rejects the result and the United States and many governments dispute it.

figures.name: Hugo Chavez
figures.role: President, founder of the Bolivarian Revolution
figures.blurb: Army officer who led a failed 1992 coup, won the presidency in 1998, and reshaped Venezuela through the Bolivarian Revolution, a polarising oil-funded socialist project, until his death from cancer in 2013.

figures.name: Nicolas Maduro
figures.blurb: Chavez's chosen successor, narrowly elected in 2013, under whom Venezuela suffered a severe economic collapse and mass emigration, and whose 2024 re-election was widely disputed.

pullquote.text: By late 2024 more than 7.7 million Venezuelans had left the country, one of the largest displacement crises in the world.
pullquote.attribution: United Nations refugee agency (UNHCR) and the R4V platform

figures.name: Simon Bolivar
figures.role: El Libertador, liberator of South America
figures.blurb: The Caracas-born hero of South American independence, who liberated Venezuela and several other nations and founded Gran Colombia, and after whom the Bolivarian Republic is named.

figures.name: Francisco de Miranda
figures.blurb: El Precursor, the revolutionary who first sought independence for Spanish America and led Venezuela's First Republic before dying in a Spanish prison.

figures.name: Hugo Chavez
figures.role: President, founder of the Bolivarian Revolution
figures.blurb: Dominant figure of modern Venezuela, who launched the oil-funded Bolivarian Revolution that reshaped and polarised the nation from 1999 until his death in 2013.
